coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots enhanced polyurethane-urea nanocomposites with mechanical reinforcement and room-temperature self-healing performance
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yifei
Xu, Shuxin
Liu, Jiarui
Zhang, Kaiyuan
Guan, Yu
Li, Shaoxiang
coredata.dc:description: Developing high-performance polymer composites with self-healing and recyclable performance is crucial for long-term durability of the devices. This paper presents a novel method by incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the chain extension reaction of polymers to construct self-healing polyurethane-urea (PUU) with excellent integrated mechanical and multi-recyclable properties. By the introduction of the CQDs with surface containing ample amide, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups, the cross-linking networks are formed, and simultaneously, abundant H-bonds at the interface between CQDs nanospheres and PUU matrix provide strong interfacial interactions, which endows material reinforcement and self-healing effect. The addition of a very low amount of CQDs (0.15 wt%) can significantly improves the mechanical performance, especially the tensile stress and toughness, which increased about 74.4 % (35.2 MPa to 61.4 MPa) and 115.2 % (34.8 MJ·m−3 to 74.9 MJ·m−3), respectively. Besides, unlike other nanofillers, the addition of CQDs (more than 1 wt%) enables the PUU with excellent self-healing and recycling properties, and for 3 wt% addition, the room temperature self-healing efficiency can even achieve 97.0 % for 24 h without external intervention. Furthermore, the addition of the CQDs can also significantly the damping effect of the polymers. It is highly anticipated that the research will provide a facile method to construct high performance polymer materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polyurethane-urea
CQDs
Mechanical reinforcement
Self-healing
Recyclability

National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52304232 ), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2020QE134 ), the Shandong Provincial Major Science and Technology Innovation Program ( 2022CXGC020401 ), and the Taishan Industrial Experts Program .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of CQDs synthesis route. (b) The FT-IR spectra of the CQDs. (c) TEM image of CQDs. (d) SEM images of cross-sections of PUU, PUU-CD0.15 and PUU-CD3 (e) the DLS results of CQDs.
(a) FT-IR curves of the PUU and PUU-CDx. (b) FT-IR curve of the reaction between CQDs and MDI-50.

(a) Digital photograph of PUU and PUU-CDx. (b) Strain-stress curves of PUU and PUU-CDx. (c) Elastic storage modulus and (d) the loss tanget versus temperature of PUU and PUU-CDx. (e) Schematic diagram of the chemical structure of PUU-CDx. (f) XRD pattern of PUU and PUU-CDx. (g) AFM height sensor images of PUU, PUU-CD0.15 and PUU-CD3.

(a) The tensile stress–strain curves of PUU-CD3 after the first, second, and third self-healing cycles at room temperature for 24 h. (b,c) Temperature-dependent FT-IR of CO from 30 to 100 °C for PUU (b) and PUU-CD3 (c). (d,e) FT-IR spectrum of CO of PUU (d) and PUU-CD3 (e) with peak deconvolution. (f) Digital photos of the self-healing behavior of PUU-CD3 at room temperature.

(a) The hot-press recycling process of PUU-CD2. (b) Tensile stress–strain curves of the PUU-CD2 elastomer for the first, second, and third cycles of hot-press recycling process under a pressure of 4 MPa at 80 °C. (c) The solvent recycling process of PUU-CD2. (d) Tensile stress–strain curves of the PUU-CD2 elastomer for the first, second, and third solvent recycling.
Statistical data on the mechanical properties of PUU and PUU-CDx.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots enhanced polyurethane-urea nanocomposites with mechanical reinforcement and room-temperature self-healing performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Safety Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, 266042, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment and Safety Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment and Safety Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology, 266042, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing high-performance polymer composites with self-healing and recyclable performance is crucial for long-term durability of the devices. This paper presents a novel method by incorporating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the chain extension reaction of polymers to construct self-healing polyurethane-urea (PUU) with excellent integrated mechanical and multi-recyclable properties. By the introduction of the CQDs with surface containing ample amide, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups, the cross-linking networks are formed, and simultaneously, abundant H-bonds at the interface between CQDs nanospheres and PUU matrix provide strong interfacial interactions, which endows material reinforcement and self-healing effect. The addition of a very low amount of CQDs (0.15 wt%) can significantly improves the mechanical performance, especially the tensile stress and toughness, which increased about 74.4 % (35.2 MPa to 61.4 MPa) and 115.2 % (34.8 MJ·m−3 to 74.9 MJ·m−3), respectively. Besides, unlike other nanofillers, the addition of CQDs (more than 1 wt%) enables the PUU with excellent self-healing and recycling properties, and for 3 wt% addition, the room temperature self-healing efficiency can even achieve 97.0 % for 24 h without external intervention. Furthermore, the addition of the CQDs can also significantly the damping effect of the polymers. It is highly anticipated that the research will provide a facile method to construct high performance polymer materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Polyurethane-Urea composite materials are an immensely versatile family of polymers, which is wide used for the engineering applications, such as coatings, electronic skins and soft robots. Enhancement of the mechanical performance of the Polyurethane-Urea is always a concern [1–3]. Besides, Polymer materials are susceptible to damages due to the external factors, such as mechanical influence, and it will significantly degrade the mechanical properties of the material and shorten its lifespan, resulting in a great waste of resources [4]. Therefore, the development of materials with self-healing and recyclable abilities also attracts great attention.

For development of the advanced materials, significant efforts have been made. In the current studies, the fabrication of nanocomposites by incorporating nanoparticles into a polymer matrix through simple and controllable methods is an effective way to improve the mechanical properties [5]. Nanofillers, in comparison with conventional fillers, are characterized by their smaller size, leading to constraints or specific effects that are unique to the nanoscale. Their key property is the significant surface area, which compensates for the generally weaker interactions with polymers and offers the potential to disrupt molecular structures and modify behavior [6–9]. These situations open up possibilities for improving the micro phase separation structure of polyurethane-urea by incorporating specially tailored nano-fillers. Abrisham et al. [10] simultaneously added multi-walled carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and montmorillonite (MMT) into thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU). When both CNTs and MMT were added at a concentration of 2.5 wt%, the tensile strength of the composite material reached 31.7 MPa, which represented a 256 % increase compared to the original sample. Mandal et al. [11] introduced carboxylated multi-wall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) into graphene flakes as porous 3D nanofillers for filling thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU). At 20 °C, the tensile strength was improved by 300 % (5.1 MPa). Hu et al. [12] improved the impact resistance of polyurethane elastomer (PUE) by synergistic interaction between SiO2 and an ionic liquid. At a loading of 2 wt%, the dynamic compression yield strength was increased by 190 %. He et al. [13] utilized nanofunctionalized lignin nanoparticles as crosslinkers for PUE, resulting in a 13 % increase in tensile strength and a 24 % increase in elongation at break. Gupta et al. [14] employed reduced graphene oxide (RGO) as a reinforcing material for polyurethane, resulting in a 129 % increase in nanoscale indentation elastic modulus compared to pure PU. However, the above nano-modified materials, the addition of nano-filler did not show any self-healing effect. Current promising self-healing methods include reversible covalent bonds(such as Diels-Alder bonds [15], disulfide bonds [16], etc) and reversible non-covalent bonds(like hydrogen bonds [17], coordination bonds [18], etc), and dynamic reversible hydrogen bonds due to the lower bond energy (about one-tenth the energy of a covalent bond) can easily break and reform, meanwhile, it can still facilitate formation of the crystallinity, and phase separation structure of the polymer which can improve the mechanical properties. In addition, the lower hydrogen bond energy allows the materials to restore the protective/recycle abilities under room conditions after accidental damage [19]. Therefore, this paper seeks to find a new way that the nano-filler can form micro-crosslinking and rich hydrogen bonds with Polyurethane-Urea, which can improve the mechanical properties of the material while endowing it self-healing and recyclable performance even under room conditions.

In recent years, CQDs have attracted great interest due to their physical characteristics (such as photoluminescence [20,21]), photochemistry properties (such as photocatalysis [22]), biocompatibility [23]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be defined as carbon-based fluorescent nanoparticles with a size smaller than 10 nm. CQDs/polymer composites and hybrid materials have been used in sensing [24], optics [25], corrosion resistance [26], and other applications [27], while the research of its influence on the performance of the polyurethane-urea (PUU) has not yet attracted widespread attention. In fact, the surfaces of CQDs were rich in functional groups such as amide, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, which can participate in the chain extension reaction of polyurethane-urea, meanwhile the abundant surface groups also contribute to the formation of rich hydrogen bonds with the urethane and urea groups in the PUU, which can improve its self-healing and recycling performance. Furthermore, their abundant and inexpensive raw material sources and simple preparation process are suitable for scale production application [28,29]. Therefore, it is interesting to research the performance changes of the CQDs composite PUU materials.

Considering the special characters of the CQDs, this study developed a novel modification method for the PUU polymer by incorporating the CQDs. The mechanical and self-healing properties of the nanocomposite polymer PUU-CDx were studied. The addition of just 0.05 wt% CQDs can significantly improve the mechanical properties of the original sample, elevating tensile strength and toughness by 55.1 % and 78.4 %, respectively, and at a concentration of 0.15 wt%, the mechanical properties reach a highest level, with increases of 74.4 % in tensile strength (61.4 MPa) and 115.2 % in toughness (74.9 MJ·m−3). In addition, unlike other nanofillers, with the increase of the CQDs, the PUU-CDx show the self-healing property, with the addition of 2 wt%, the polymer can achieve a healing efficiency around 96.2 % at 80 °C for 24 h, while maintaining high mechanical strength (41.6 MPa), and it also possesses near to 100 % recyclability. For the 3 wt% addition of the CQDs, the room temperature self-healing can be achieved, with the elongation at break increased about 206.7 % (891.7 %), and the healing efficiency can reach to 97.0 % for 24 h without the need for external intervention. The addition of the CQDs can also significantly the damping effect of the polymers, which indicated it has a good application prospect in the field of damping. This approach provides a novel way for the creation of high-performance polyurethane-urea materials.

MDI-50 was obtained from Wanhua Chemical Group CO.Ltd, China. Polytetramethylene ether glycol (PTMEG, Mn≈1000) and 4,4′-methylenebis(N-sec-butylaniline) (MDBA) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). Urea and Citric acid (CA) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Reagent (China). Acetone (AC, AR grade, dry with molecular sieves, water ≤ 50 ppm) and Trichloromethane (TCM, AR grade, dry with molecular sieves, water ≤ 50 ppm) were purchased from Xilong Chemical Reagent (China). N,N-Dimethylacetamide (DMAc, AR grade, dry with molecular sieves, water ≤ 50 ppm) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (China). All the other chemicals were used as received unless otherwise stated.

CQDs were prepared according to a modified literature procedure [30]. In detail, citric acid (5 g) and urea (10 g) were added to DMAc (50 mL) to form a transparent solution. The solution was reacted at 175 °C for 6 h and then cooled to room temperature. The obtained dark brown solution was concentrated in vacuum at 70 °C for 6 h to remove the water produced by the reaction. The concentrated product was centrifuged at 13 000r·min−1 for 40 min at 5 °C. The precipitate was collected, dispersed in DI water and centrifuged (13 000r·min−1, 5 °C, 30 min) twice to wash off residual DMAc, and then freeze-dried to give the dark products of CQDs.

The preparation of all samples is usually divided into two parts. In the first part, separate AC solutions were prepared for MDI-50 (5 g, 20 mmol) and PTMEG (10 g, 10 mmol) with a concentration of 75 % (w/v). The two components underwent a reaction at 80 °C for 2 h. Consequently, isocyanate terminated prepolymer was produced. Then, an AC solution of MDI-50 (1.13 g, 4.51 mmol) with a concentration of 75 % (w/v) was directly added to the system, followed by the gradual addition of an AC solution of MDBA (3.87 g, 12.49 mmol) with a concentration of 75 % (w/v). The reaction was carried out at 80 °C for 2 h, then the solution of PUU was obtained. All the above reactions were carried out under nitrogen protection.

In the second part, a certain amount of CQDs were ultrasonically dispersed in 20 mL TCM for 1 h, and then mixed with the PUU solution in a nitrogen atmosphere for a violent ultrasonic mechanical stirring for 30 min, and it should be noted that the NCO group was over-added in order for the CQDs to participate in the reaction. After that, the solution was poured into a horizontal PTFE mold, vacuum dried at 80 °C for 24 h, and then dried at 80 °C for 72 h. The prepared polyurethane-urea/CQDs composites were named as PUU-CDx, where x = 0.05–3 wt%, x means the percentage of the added carbon dot mass to the PUU mass.

The FTIR samples were prepared by spreading the final solution onto the surface of the potassium bromide salt tablet. The postprocessing of the FTIR samples were the same as the sheet samples. A Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrophotometer was applied to collect FTIR spectra which was recorded with a resolution of 2 cm−1. In order to guarantee the thickness of samples in accordance with Beer- Lambert law, the samples should be sufficiently thin. For the temperature-dependent FTIR scans, a heating cell connected to a temperature controller was used with the temperature range from 30 °C to 100 °C. After spectra collection, the curve fitting procedure was conducted by the specific Software “Peak Fit v4.12”, where Gaussian function was applied. Meanwhile, the integral areas of curves were obtained by means of peak resolving and the mean square error deviations of all the data were smaller than 0.01.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements (5400MC, PerkinElmer, Inc., Waltham, USA) were performed by using AlKa (1486.6 eV) radiation operating at 150 W (15 kV, 10 mA). From the narrow-range measurements, C1s, N1s, and O1s spectra were obtained. The X-ray incidence angle was 458. All spectra were compensated by assuming that the C1s CC/CH peak appeared at a binding energy of 285.0 eV. The spectra were decomposed by fitting mixed Gaussian-Lorentzian functions, according to expected chemical assignments. Perform curve fitting using the “XPSPEAK41″ data analysis software.

Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (WAXD) profiles were determined at room temperature using a Rigaku DMAX-Rapid micro-diffractometer equipped with curved image plate detector. The X-ray beam was generated from a Cu point-focused source (λ = 0.154 nm) at 50 kV and 40 mA. 2D isotropic scattering patterns were integrated to give 1D profiles in a 2θ range from 5 to 80°.

Morphologies of specimens after different treatments were characterized using a JSM 7500F scanning electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 5.0 kV. Before scanning electron microscopy (SEM) characterization, the surface of each sample was pretreated by coating it with a thin layer of gold.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) tests were carried out with a Hitachi HT-7700 microscope (Tokyo, Japan) operating at 80 kV.
The diameter of particle was characterized by the Dynamic light scattering tests (DLS) (NS-90Z, Omicron Instrument Zhuhai Co.).

Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) experiments were carried out on a DTGA-60 (SHIMADZU) analyzer to measure the thermal stability of materials from the onset temperatures of thermal degradation. The samples were heated from 50 °C to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under N2 purge.
Tensile tests were carried out using a CMT-4204 electric tensile tester (Shenzhen, China). The average values for at least five specimens were reported for all tests.

The Dynamic mechanical (DMA) tests were carried out using DMA Q800 produced by TA Instruments Co., Ltd. in the United States. The samples were usually cut into 40 mm × 4 mm × 2 mm splines using tensile mode, with a temperature range of −120 °C to 100 °C, an amplitude of 0.15 %, a frequency of 1 Hz, and a heating rate of 3 °C/min.

Tapping mode atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements were performed on a Bruker Icon atomic force microscope at ambient temperature. The cantilevers used in this study were Nanosensors PPP-NCH AFM probes with spring constant 10–130 N and resonance frequency 204–497 kHz. The tip radius of curvature was reported to be less than 10 nm. All phase images reported were from scan areas of 5 × 5 μm2 and acquired using tapping forces, rsp (set point amplitude/free amplitude of oscillation) between 0.7 and 0.9.

The CQDs were synthesized through the dehydration reaction of urea and citric acid in DMAc solvent, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The surface structure of the CQDs was characterized by FT-IR (Fig. 1b). Absorption bands in the range of 3600–3300 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of NH. Absorption bands from 3250 to 3000 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibration of OH. Absorption bands at 1730–1500 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibration of CO. Absorption bands from 1420 to 1320 cm−1 were assigned to bending vibration of CH2 [20,21,31]. The chemical bonding information of the CQDs was further revealed by XPS measurements (Fig. S1). XPS spectra of the CQDs showed three peaks at 530.6, 400.0, and 284.0 eV, which were attributed to O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively. The C1s spectrum of CQDs showed five peaks at 283.3, 283.9, 284.5, 286.0, and 287.4 eV that were attributed to sp2C, sp3C, CN, CO, and CO, respectively. The N1s spectrum of CQDs showed three peaks at 389.1, 398.9, and 400.3 eV, which were attributed to NCC, N (C)3, and NH bands, respectively. The O1s spectrum of CQDs showed two peaks at 530.1 and 531.5 eV, which were attributed to CO and CO bands, respectively [20,31–33]. The results demonstrated abundant amide, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups, which could participate in the chain extension reaction of polyurethane-urea and provide a large number of sites for hydrogen bond formation. The XRD patterns of the CQDs (Fig. S2) displayed a strong diffraction peak at 3.4 Å. It was attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms similar to graphite lattice spacing [34,35]. The micro-morphology of the prepared CQDs (aqueous dispersed) was further investigated by using TEM, displayed in the Fig. 1c. It was found that CQDs indicated a spherical shape and uniformly dispersed. The cross-sections of PUU, PUU-CD0.15, and PUU-CD3 were also analyzed shown in Fig. 1d, and it could also be seen that there were no obvious differences compared with each other, and it did not show the agglomeration of the CQDs. This could be the good dispersion in itself that was in rich functional groups, and the reaction between the groups NH2 and OH of the CQDs with NCO groups as discussed in Section 3.2 could also promote its dispersion. The particle size distribution of CQDs was further tested by the DLS testing (Fig. 1e), which showed the average particle size of CQDs was 5.1 nm. Fig. S3 showed optical images of CQDs illuminated by white light (left, fluorescent lamp) and ultraviolet (UV) light (right, 365 nm). The CQDs aqueous solution appeared black under sunlight and yellow-green under UV light irradiation.

Typical synthesis route for PUU-CDx was shown in Fig. S4. The infrared spectra of all samples at room temperature were shown in Fig. 2 a. It could be seen that there was no significant difference in the infrared characteristic peaks of the samples at room temperature, and the peaks at 3430 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibration of free NH. The absorption peaks at 1730 cm−1 and 1670 cm−1 were the CO stretching vibration peaks in urethane group and urea group, respectively [36,37]. The broad peaks near 3300 cm−1 were assigned to stretching vibration of H-bonds NH. It should be noted that in the samples containing CQDs, the NCO group was over-added, but the NCO group was not observed at 2260 cm−1, and it could be inferred that the CQDs reacted with the NCO group which played a certain role in the micro-crosslinking. To verify the CQDs involvement in the reaction, the experiment of MDI-50 reaction with CQDs were set up, and the results were shown in Fig. 2b. The presence of peaks at 1780 cm−1 (CO stretch vibrations, amide) and the absorption bands from 3250 to 3000 cm−1 (OH stretch vibrations) indicated the incomplete reaction of amide and hydroxyl groups on the CQDs surfaces [38,39]. However, the peaks at 1730 cm−1 (CO stretch vibrations, ester), 1230 cm−1 (COC stretch vibrations, ester), and 1640 cm−1 (CO stretch vibrations, urea) confirmed the successful formation of the urethane and urea groups, and the disappearance of the peak position of isocyanate at 2260 cm−1 indicated that MDI-50 can react completely with CQDs, which further proved the CQDs participating in the PUU chain extension reaction. As shown in Fig. S5, PUU-CD0.15 emitted blue light when exposed to UV light (365 nm).

The thermal stability of all samples was tested by TGA in N2 atmosphere and in the range of 50–700 °C (Fig. S6a). The detailed information is summarized in Table S1. The temperature of 5 % weight loss (T5%) of all samples were above 270 °C. The DTG curve shown in Fig. S6b indicated that all samples had three decomposition temperatures (Tmax1, Tmax2 and Tmax3), suggesting that the thermal decomposition process of all samples occurred in three stages. The first degradation step occurred at around 310 °C, which could be attributed to the thermal cracking of the urethane groups [40]. The second decomposition step was around 360 °C and could be allocated to the thermal decomposition of urea bonds [41]. The third step of degradation, occurring at around 415 °C, was due to the breakage of the polyol chain segment [42].

In order to understand the effect of CQDs on the mechanical properties of PUU, the samples with different concentration of the CQDs were prepared as shown in Fig. 3 a, and the amount of the materials used in the synthesis process are supplied in Table S2. The mechanical properties of the PUU/PUU-CDx were characterized by tensile tests at a stretching speed of 500 mm/min, as shown in Fig. 3b, and the detailed testing data are shown in Table 1 . The stress–strain curve showed that for the pure PUU sample, the tensile stress at break was 35.2 MPa with the elongation at break of 431.0 %, the toughness and tensile modulus were 34.8 MJ·m−3 and 33.0 MPa, respectively. With the increase of the CQDs from 0.05 to 0.15 wt%, all of the mechanical properties were improved, especially the tensile stress at break and toughness. Compared to the PUU, the tensile stress at break and toughness of the PUU-CD0.05 increased by 55.1 % (54.6 MPa) and 78.4 % (62.1 MJ·m−3), respectively, and for the PUU-CD0.15, they increased 74.4 % (61.4 MPa) and 115.2 % (74.9 MJ·m−3). It is interesting to note that, compared with the other nanofillers [43–46], very small additions about 0.15 wt% of the total mass of the CQDs almost doubled the tensile stress at break and toughness. At the same time, the elongation at break and the tensile modulus also increased 11.1 % and 20.3 %, respectively. However, as the amount of the CDQs continued to increase from 0.15 to 1 wt%, all of the mechanical properties, except for the elongation at break in the Table 1 decreased, but for PUU-CD2 the tensile stress at break (41.6 MPa) and the toughness (65.3 MJ·m−3) increased, and the elongation at break (531.5 %) and the tensile modulus (19.2 MPa) decreased. For PUU-CD3, the tensile stress at break (4.2 MPa), toughness (16.1 MJ·m3), tensile modulus (0.9 MPa) greatly decreased with the elongation at break (891.7 %).

The temperature-dependent storage modulus, loss modulus and tanδ were measured by dynamic mechanical analysis (DMA). As shown in Fig. 3c-d, the storage modulus decreased with the increase of the temperature, and the tanδ curves also showed distinct peaks between 20 and 40 °C, which indicated the values of the glass transition temperature. The results showed that the glass transition temperature increased with the addition of the CQDs from 0 to 0.15 wt%. This increase could be related with the degree of crosslinking and hydrogen-bond interaction, as described in Fig. 3e. The increase in CQDs could promote the micro-crosslinking of the PUU-CDx, which enhanced the hard segment domains by forming urethane/urea groups, and hydrogen-bond formed by the interaction of the surface groups of CQDs with PUU also played a certain role, which explained the increase of the tensile stress at break, toughness, tensile modulus and elongation at break. However, when the amount of the CQDs added more than 0.15 wt% and less than 1 wt%, with further addition of CQDs, the glass transition temperature gradually decreased, and it could be that the increase of CQDs might blend more soft chain segments into hard segments micro-regions, and promote the mobility of the chain in the PUU which reduced the tensile stress at break, toughness, tensile modulus and improved the elongation. The AFM analysis also showed the change in microphase separation structure as discussed below. When the amount of CQDs added more than 1 wt%, the glass transition temperature of PUU-CD2 was higher than PUU-CD1. with the increase of CQDs, It could be that the density of the hydrogen bond increased, and the high content of hydrogen bond promoted the mechanical properties by the non-covalent bonding force, which improved the tensile stress at break and the toughness for PUU-CD2. However, an apparent decrease of the glass transition temperature could be seen for PUU-CD3, this could be due to the change of microphase separation as substantial increase of the CQDs which further promote the interfusion between hard and soft segments and improve the chain mobility, the higher value of the tanδ of PUU-CD1 to 3 verified the higher chain mobility, so the increase of the CQDs could greatly improve the elongation at break and reduce the glass transition temperature, tensile stress, toughness, tensile modulus. This could also be the reason for that the glass transition temperature of the PUU-CD2 was higher than PUU-CD0 to 1, but its self-healing performance was better than PUU-CD0 to 0.2, and it was the result of a combination of the hydrogen bond and the chain mobility. The addition of the CQDs enhanced the non-covalent bonding force which endowed the material PUU-CD1,2 or 3 self-healing and reproducing properties, and PUU-CD3 could achieve the self-healing under room temperature, and it would be discussed in the Section 3.4. What’s more, the characteristics of the viscoelastic state were gradually apparent during the process, and the DMA curves also showed that the addition of the CQDs broadened the peak of tanδ increasing the damping capacity, and this indicates that the introduction of CQDs into the PUU system can effectively dissipate the energy under tensile stress, which indicated the prospect of its application in damping materials. Furthermore, the increase of density of the hydrogen bond.

For further analysis, XRD and AFM tests were conducted on all of the samples. PUU-CDx exhibited a typical amorphous X-ray diffraction profile, with two peaks observed around 20 and 41 degrees, as shown in Fig. 3f. These peaks were attributed to the orientation and ordered arrangement of the molecular chain segments. It was clear that the peak intensity of PUU-CDx initially increased with the addition of CQDs form 0 to 0.15 wt%, and the highest intensity was observed for PUU-CD0.15. This verified that the addition of the CQDs played a cross-linking role and promoted the aggregation of hard segments to form ordered structure. However, as the content of CQDs continued to increase, the peak intensity decreased, indicating that CQDs also played a role in blending soft segment into hard segment domains. Interestingly, although the tensile stress at break and toughness of PUU-CD2 improved due to the non-covalent bonding force of hydrogen bonds, the peak intensity did not increase. This indicated that the hydrogen bonds formed by the addition of CQDs were irregular, contributing to the self-healing properties as well. The microphase-separated structure of the PUU-CDx was examined by the AFM, as shown in Fig. 3g and Fig. S7. The red and yellow region represent the hard chain segment aggregation domains, while the black and purple regions represent the soft chain segment aggregation domains. The green regions represents the mixed domains between the hard chain segment and the soft chain segment. Initially, the addition of the CQDs (from 0 to 0.15w%) promoted the formation of a microphase-separated structure, with a clear boundary between soft and hard domains observed for the PUU-CDx, which improved the mechanical performance. With further addition of the CQDs, the microphase separation structure further changed, and for PUU-CD1, there was a tendency for the island phases to fuse together. This could be attributed to the formation of the irregular hydrogen-bonds and the blending of soft and hard chain segments. To a certain degree, for PUU-CD2 the formation of hydrogen-bonds enhanced the tensile stress and toughness. As the content of CQDs continued to rise, the island phases began to interconnect and fuse, indicating that soft chain segments were blended into the hard chain segments, enhancing their compatibility and weakened the microphase separation. The restrictions on the movement of soft chain segments imposed by the hard chain segments gradually weakened. The results further explained the changes of the mechanical properties.

Therefore, based on the above analysisi, the changes in mechanical properties of PUU-CDx were the combined result of the micro-crosslinking effect, hydrogen-bond, and alteration of the microphase separation structure induced by CQDs. On the one hand, the CQDs reacted with NCO group, which could generate the micro-crosslinking, and the hydrogen-bond formed by the interaction of the surface groups of CQDs with PUU could also play a certain role in the mechanical properties. What’s more, as a non-covalent bond, it could improve the self-healing performance of the material. On the other hand, the CQDs as nanometer materials played a role in promoting the blending of soft segments into hard segments, which altered the microphase separation structure.

In order to further investigate the role of CQDs in the composite material, continuous loading–unloading measurements were performed for 5 cycles on samples PUU, PUU-CD0.15 and PUU-CD2. The results are shown in Fig. S8. In the first loading–unloading cycle at 100 % strain, the rebound rates of samples PUU, PUU-CD0.15 and PUU-CD2 were 87.2 %, 82.5 %, and 64.7 %, respectively. They exhibited dissipated energies of 0.52 MJ·m−3, 0.61 MJ·m−3, and 0.91 MJ·m−3, respectively. It was observed that with the increase in CQDs, the rapid rebound ability of the sample gradually weakens, while the dissipated energy of the sample gradually increased, indicating that CQDs enhanced the energy absorption performance of PUU. In the second loading–unloading cycle, which was performed immediately after the first loading–unloading cycle, there was a significant decrease in the hysteresis loop area. This decreasing trend gradually slowed down in the subsequent continuous cycles. They all exhibited a typical Mullins effect, which was the stress softening behavior caused by hysteresis effects such as microstructure destruction, dissociation of H-bonds, and orientation of hard segment domains [47–49]. These results further confirmed that the addition of CQDs greatly improved the damping performance of PUU, indicating its promising application prospects in the field of damping.

As described above, with the addition of the CQDs (in amounts equal to or greater than 1 wt%), PUU-CDx showed the ability of the self-healing behavior. To investigate the self-healing behavior, dumbbell-shaped samples of PUU-CDx (4 mm × 75 mm × 2 mm) were used. In the self-healing experiments, the sample was cut into two pieces, and the two cut surfaces were allowed to naturally come into contact with each other without applying any external force. The tests were repeated three times. For PUU-CD1,2 and 3, the samples were able to achieve self-healing under 80 °C for 24 h. As shown in Fig. S9 and Fig. 4 a, the tensile tests showed that the PUU-CD1, PUU-CD2 and PUU-CD3 demonstrated healing efficiencies of approximately 71.7 %, 96.2 % and 98.7 %, respectively. The self-healing ability significantly increased with the addition of CQDs. For PUU-CD3, it could even achieve self-healing with the high healing efficiency around 97.0 % under room temperature for 24 h. These results demonstrated that the addition of CQDs endowed the PUU with self-healing properties. The photograph in Fig. 4f visually illustrates the outstanding self-healing characteristics of sample PUU-CD3, which exhibits excellent tensile performance after 30 min of healing at room temperature. In order to demonstrate the self-healing properties of the materials more effectively, we compared the scratch repair capabilities of PUU, PUU-CD2, and PUU-CD3, as shown in Fig. S10. From the results, it could be observed that PUU did not possess scratch repair capability, while PUU-CD3 exhibited higher scratch repair efficiency compared to PUU-CD2. For PUU-CD2 and PUU-CD3 the scratch healed at the fortieth and twentieth minute under 80 °C, respectively.

In order to understand the mechanism of the CQDs effect on the self-healing performance, the temperature-dependent FTIR were further investigated for the PUU and PUU-CD3 samples, and the infrared characteristic peaks were shown in Fig. 4b–c. There were two types of CO peaks: one from the urethane group at around 1728 cm−1 and the other from the urea group at around 1667 cm−1. The result showed that the CO peak of urea group underwent a redshift (moving towards the larger wave numbers), and the CO peaks of the urethane group did not shift during the heating process for PUU sample. However, both the CO peaks of the urethane group and the urea group were red shifted in the PUU-CD3 sample. In addition, the onset temperature of the CO (urea group) peak shift in the PUU-CD3 sample was lower than that in the PUU sample. This redshift in the CO peaks was due to the formation of the hydrogen-bond, and it changed the intergroup interaction forces [50,51]. Through variable-temperature infrared analysis of the CO infrared peak values, it was observed that as the content of CQDs gradually increased, the CO groups (urethane group) in the soft segments were also capable of forming hydrogen bonds, which indicated that the addition of CQDs facilitated the formation of the hydrogen-bonds, as more hydrogen bonding sites could form between the CQDs and PUU, and more soft segments were blending into the hard segments, enabling the CO groups (urethane group) in the soft segments to also form hydrogen bonding, which significantly improved hydrogen bonding density, and it also explained the fact that the onset temperature of the CO (urea group) peak shift reducing, as the non-covalent hydrogen-bond density increased, the less power was required to enhanced the molecular motion, and it was consistent with AFM analysis that the increasing CQDs enlarged the transitional phase area led to a certain degree of merging in the island phase, and the compatibility between soft and hard segments were raised that promoted the movement ability of the chain segments, so the room temperature self-healing ability of the PUU-CDx was greatly improved. To verify the increase in hydrogen-bond density of the PUU and PUU-CDx due to the addition of the CQDs, the infrared curves for different content of CQDs in PUU-CDx were shown in Fig. 4d–e and Fig. S11. The CO stretching vibration absorption bands of all samples were analyzed with peak fitting, which were divided into seven sub-peaks, and the deconvoluted sub-peaks have been attributed to the free and hydrogen-bonded CO in the urethane and urea groups. The quantification parameters of PUU and PUU-CDx were shown in Table S3 [52,53]. With the increase in the content of CQDs, the hydrogen bonding degree of the samples gradually increased from 62.93 % to 74.33 %. These results further demonstrated that the addition of CQDs efficiently improved the formation of the hydrogen-bonds, and the non-covalent interaction caused by hydrogen bonding density greatly enhanced the self-healing performance of the PUU-CDx.

The high dynamic nature of hydrogen bonding also imparted excellent recyclability and reshaping ability to PUU-CDx samples under mild conditions. Taking PUU-CD2 as an example, the shredded small pieces of PUU-CD2 sample were hot-pressed at 80 °C and 4 MPa pressure for 1 h, resulting in the formation of a new and complete material (Fig. 5 a). It can be observed from the stress–strain curve in Fig. 5b that the recycled PUU-CD2 maintained its original mechanical properties even after undergoing three cycles of shredding and recycling processes, indicating remarkable recyclability. In addition to hot-press reshaping, PUU-CD2 could also be reshaped using a solvent-assisted method (Fig. 5c). The PUU-CD2 sample could be completely dissolved in DMAc, and then cast into a PTFE evaporating dish and dried under vacuum. After complete drying at 80 °C, the original defect-free PUU-CD2 sample could be regenerated. After three solvent-assisted recovery processes, there was no significant change in the mechanical properties of the PUU-CD2 compared to the original sample (Fig. 5d). In summary, these results indicated the addition of CQDs endowed the PUU excellent recyclability, and provided a new idea for the preparation of recyclable materials.

In conclusion, a series of PUU-CDx composite elastomers were synthesized to study the impact of CQDs on the performance of PUU. At low CQDs addition levels, it acted as an excellent cross-linker, reacting with the isocyanate group and significantly enhancing the mechanical properties of PUU. For an addition of 0.15 wt% CQDs, the tensile stress at break, toughness, elongation at break, and tensile modulus increased by approximately 74.4 % (61.4 MPa), 115.2 % (74.9 MJ·m−3), 11.1 % (478.8 %), and 20.3 % (39.7 MPa), respectively. Unlike other nanofillers, an increase in CQDs promoted the formation of hydrogen bonds and increased the density, resulting in exceptional self-healing and recyclability properties for PUU-CDx. With a 2 wt% addition of CQDs, the polymer achieved a healing efficiency of approximately 96.2 % at 80 °C for 24 h, while maintaining high mechanical strength (41.6 MPa), and demonstrated close to 100 % recyclability. Additionally, room temperature self-healing was accomplished with a CQDs addition exceeding 3 wt%, achieving a healing efficiency of 97.0 % without external intervention, accompanied by a significant increase in elongation at break of approximately 206.7 % (891.7 %). The formation of high-density hydrogen bonds also contributed to enhanced energy dissipation, as evidenced by the considerable improvement in the damping performance of the polymer with the addition of CQDs. We believe that the proposed novel strategy of designing PUU-CDx composite elastomers holds vast potential for engineering protection and extending the lifespan of polymer materials.

Yifei Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shuxin Xu: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Jiarui Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Kaiyuan Zhang: Validation, Project administration. Yu Guan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Shaoxiang Li: Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully appreciate the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52304232), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (Grant No. ZR2020QE134), the Shandong Provincial Major Science and Technology Innovation Program (2022CXGC020401), and the Taishan Industrial Experts Program.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
Supporting Information for Carbon Quantum Dots Enhanced Polyurethane-Urea Nanocomposites with Mechanical Reinforcement and Room-Temperature Self-Healing Performance.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Liu, Y. He, J. Leng, Shape Memory Supramolecular Polyurea with Adjustable Toughness and Ultrahigh Energy Density, ACS Applied Polymer Materials, 4 (2022) 6092-6102.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Eom, S.-M. Kim, M. Lee, H. Jeon, J. Park, E.S. Lee, S.Y. Hwang, J. Park, D.X. Oh, Mechano-responsive hydrogen-bonding array of thermoplastic polyurethane elastomer captures both strength and self-healing, Nature Communications, 12 (2021).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Towards mechanical robust yet self-healing polyurethane elastomers via combination of dynamic main chain and dangling quadruple hydrogen bonds
